ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated matcha code; A00LC (Green tea) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT does not have a dedicated matcha code; 227313005 (Green tea) is the closest defensible parent.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of prepared matcha tea made with approximately 2 g of matcha powder whisked into 240 ml of hot water, as commonly served in cafes and tea houses.

nutrient_source.title: Beverages, tea, green, brewed, regular — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #14197; Matcha powder nutrient profile cross-referenced with USDA Foundation Foods
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are based on USDA FoodData Central data for brewed green tea (SR Legacy #14197) and matcha powder (FDC #2003571). Per-100g values reflect the prepared beverage form as served. Matcha powder is approximately 2 g per 240 ml serving; per-100g values for the prepared drink are derived accordingly. Core macros are very low due to high water content of the prepared beverage.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — Green tea / matcha beverage
gi_source.source_match_notes: Matcha as a prepared beverage contains negligible digestible carbohydrates (approximately 0.2 g per 100 g). No direct GI test exists for matcha beverage in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel value) per Project Veritas convention for essentially zero-carbohydrate beverages. Glycemic load per 240 ml serving is 0.0. This is consistent with plain green tea, which is classified as GI low by convention. Reference: Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content; L-theanine and catechins may support mild vasodilation
hypertension.guidance: Matcha is very low in sodium (approximately 1 mg per 100 g prepared) and contains catechins and L-theanine that have been associated with modest blood pressure reduction in clinical studies; it is generally considered safe and potentially beneficial for individuals with hypertension when consumed without added sugar or high-sodium milk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content; EGCG may improve insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Prepared matcha beverage contains negligible digestible carbohydrates and has a glycemic index effectively at zero, making it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG) in matcha has been associated with improved insulin sensitivity in several randomised controlled trials, though individuals should avoid adding sugar or sweetened syrups.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High catechin and antioxidant content; negligible saturated fat and cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Matcha is rich in catechins, particularly EGCG, which have demonstrated LDL-cholesterol-lowering and anti-inflammatory effects in meta-analyses; the beverage contains no saturated fat or cholesterol and is considered cardioprotective when consumed as part of a balanced diet without added sugar or full-fat dairy.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Matcha (抹茶, maccha) is a finely stone-ground powder made from shade-grown Camellia sinensis leaves, originating in Japan and now widely consumed across East and Southeast Asia, including Singapore. Unlike steeped green tea, matcha involves consuming the entire leaf in powdered form, resulting in a significantly higher concentration of catechins, L-theanine, chlorophyll, and antioxidants. The prepared beverage is made by whisking 1–2 g of matcha powder with hot water (70–80°C) using a bamboo whisk (chasen), producing a frothy, vivid green drink with a characteristic umami-rich, slightly bitter, and grassy flavour profile. Culinary-grade matcha is also widely used in desserts, lattes, ice cream, and baked goods. Nutritionally, the prepared beverage is extremely low in calories (approximately 1 kcal per 100 g), with negligible carbohydrates, fat, and sodium. The glycemic index is effectively zero, making it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The high EGCG content has been associated with improved insulin sensitivity, LDL reduction, and mild antihypertensive effects in clinical literature. Matcha is naturally caffeine-containing (approximately 35 mg per 240 ml serving), which should be noted for individuals sensitive to stimulants. It is vegan, gluten-free, and halal. In Singapore, matcha is commonly served as a beverage or used as a flavouring in desserts at Japanese-influenced cafes and fusion restaurants.
common_user_queries: How many calories in matcha tea?
What is the glycemic index of matcha?
Is matcha good for blood pressure?
How is matcha different from green tea?
Is matcha good for heart health?

confidence_reason: Matcha as a prepared beverage is well-characterised in USDA FoodData Central (brewed green tea SR Legacy and matcha powder FDC entries). Nutrient values are adapted from these approved sources for the prepared drink form. GI is estimated by convention (effectively zero carbohydrate beverage) rather than from a direct GI test of matcha in the University of Sydney database. Confidence is medium rather than high because the nutrient values are adapted from component data rather than a direct assay of the prepared beverage as served at the specific restaurant.
notes: 1. The input record tagged this item under Malaysian cuisine with tags 'Curry Rice' and 'Nasi Lemak', which is inconsistent with matcha being a Japanese-origin product. The cuisine has been corrected to Japanese and region_of_origin set to Japan, as matcha is unambiguously a Japanese tea product. The field survey listing likely reflects a fusion or multi-cuisine restaurant offering matcha as a beverage item. 2. Per-100g values reflect the prepared matcha beverage (approximately 2 g matcha powder in 240 ml water). Fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for brewed green tea; fat content of the prepared beverage is below detection threshold (naturally zero for a water-based tea beverage). Sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for unsweetened prepared matcha per USDA SR Legacy. Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for the prepared beverage form (fibre remains in the undissolved powder residue and is negligible in the liquid). 3. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains negligible glycemic carbohydrates (approximately 0.2 g per 100 g); gi_category is low per Project Veritas convention for essentially zero-carbohydrate beverages. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy brewed green tea entry. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy brewed green tea entry. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy brewed green tea entry. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy brewed green tea entry. 5. Multilingual names: matcha is a Japanese-origin food; Japanese name 抹茶 is the canonical native-script form. Chinese simplified and traditional are both 抹茶 (the term was borrowed into Chinese). Malay, Tamil, and Hindi transliterations are provided as this food is served in Singapore. 6. The field survey price of SGD 8.90 likely reflects a prepared matcha drink (e.g. matcha latte or ceremonial matcha) rather than raw matcha powder.
